title: The Drone-ISR Kill Web That Broke Maneuver Warfare

source.original: General Valerii Zaluzhnyi on the Impossibility of Operational-Level Tasks Due to Technological Progress — Giorgi Revishvili Thread

body_html:
The Russo-Ukrainian war has become a live demonstration of what happens when ubiquitous drones, persistent ISR, and precision fires blanket a battlefield. The ability to see everything and strike almost anything has created a kill web so dense that large-scale force concentration — the prerequisite for classical maneuver warfare — becomes suicidal before it becomes decisive.

Zaluzhnyi's battlefield experience confirms what theorists have long debated: when both sides possess cheap, abundant surveillance platforms and responsive fires, the traditional cycle of mass-maneuver-breakthrough collapses. Forces that try to concentrate are detected within minutes and engaged before they can achieve momentum. The offense-defense balance has tilted dramatically toward defense, not because of trenches or fortifications alone, but because of technological transparency.

This is not simply a problem of one weapon system or one domain. It is the synergistic effect of commercial drones providing real-time targeting, electronic signals being geolocated and struck, and precision munitions — from artillery to loitering munitions — being directed by distributed sensor networks. The cumulative effect is a battlefield where hiding is nearly impossible and moving in mass is punished severely.

The consequences extend well beyond Ukraine. Any military planning for high-intensity conflict must now account for a threat environment where operational surprise is extraordinarily difficult to achieve. The assumption that superior training or technology can restore freedom of maneuver may be dangerously optimistic if the underlying physics of detection and engagement have shifted permanently.

What Ukraine's war suggests is that the kill web is not a temporary battlefield condition but a structural feature of modern conflict — one that will only intensify as autonomous systems, AI-enabled targeting, and sensor proliferation continue to accelerate.
